coredata.dc:title: In situ self-assembly assisted synthesis of N-doped mesoporous hierarchical carbon aerogels-wrapped Li2ZnTi3O8 composite for high-rate lithium ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Chinese Ceramic Society. Production and hosting by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Chinese Ceramic Society. Production and hosting by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jingjing
Zhang, Wenhan
Li, Jiawei
Wang, Bin
Xu, Chao
Lai, Chunyan
coredata.dc:description: Reinforcing electronic and ionic conductivity is very important to realize the high-rate Li2ZnTi3O8 (LZTO) anode for Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). Here, we reported a synthetic strategy toward in situ growth of homodispersed Li2ZnTi3O8 on ultralight 3D carbon aerogels (CAs) via facile heat treatment and activation process taking LiOH as activating agent. Such an optimized composite obtains a specific surface area of 154.427 m2 g−1 and a high pore volume. The strong interaction between the LZTO and N-doped CAs, a highly electrical conductivity and multidimensional ion transport channels of the hybrid result in a high capacity, outstanding high-rate performance and long cycle life. As a result, the NPC-LZTO electrode (NPC-LZTO-B) delivers a commendable reversible capacity at high-rate (e.g. 162.4 mAh g−1 at 5 A g−1 (22 C) and 127.3 mAh g−1 at 10 A g−1) and durable long-term performance (capacity retention of 73% after 4500 cycles at 5 A g−1). Moreover, the kinetic analysis confirms that the feature of pseudocapacitance boosts lithium-ion storage performance at high rate. Porous carbon aerogels frame constructed LZTO nanocomposite electrodes provide a facile way to design high durable LIBs anode.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li2ZnTi3O8 anode
N-doped
Porous carbon
Chitosan
Lithium-ion batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the construction and pyrolysis of gel for NPC-LZTO composites.
(a)-(c) FESEM, (d)-(e) TEM, (f) HR-TEM images and (g) all element mapping images of NPC-LZTO-B.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectrums, (c) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and (d) pore size distributions of NPC-LZTO-A, NPC-LZTO-B, NPC-LZTO-C and P-LZTO.
(a) XPS survey of NPC-LZTO-B and P-LZTO. The high-resolution XPS of (b) C 1s and (c) N 1s of the NPC-LZTO-B composite. XPS spectra of (d) O 1s, (e) Ti 2p and (f) Zn 2p of P-LZTO and NPC-LZTO-B.

(a) The cyclic voltammetry profiles of P-LZTO and NPC-LZTO-B at a scan rate of 0.2 mV s−1. (b) Rate performance at various current density of the four LZTO samples. (C) Charge-discharge curves of NPC-LZTO-B at different current density. (d) Comparison of rate performance NPC-LZTO-B electrode with other reported LZTO composites. (e) Long cycle performance at the current of 5 A g−1 of P-LZTO and NPC-LZTO-B.
(a) EIS curves of all electrodes (the inset of (a) is the equivalent circuit diagram). (b) The relationship between Z′ and ω−1/2 in the low frequency region.

Crystal structure, compositional characterization, and morphology of NPC-LZTO-B. SEM morphology of NPC-LZTO-B (a) before cycle and (b) after 1000 cycles at 5 A g−1. (c) XRD patterns and (d) Partially enlarged XRD patterns of NPC-LZTO-B after 1000 cycles at 5 A g−1. (e) Schematic illustration showing the energy storage mechanism involved of N-doped porous CAs-LZTO electrode.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of NPC-LZTO-B and (b) P-LZTO at diverse scan rates range from 0.2 to 1.2 mV s−1. (c) The relationship plots between peak current (ip) and ν1/2. (d) The log (scan rate)-log (peak current curve) of each peak and the scan rate from 0.2 to 1.2 mV s−1. (e) CV profile with shaded portion showing the capacitive contribution at 1.2 mV s−1. (f) The pseudocapacitive contribution (in percentage) at different scan rates.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of The Chinese Ceramic Society.

head.ce:title: In situ self-assembly assisted synthesis of N-doped mesoporous hierarchical carbon aerogels-wrapped Li2ZnTi3O8 composite for high-rate lithium ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai, 200090, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power Shanghai University of Electric Power

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai, 200090, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reinforcing electronic and ionic conductivity is very important to realize the high-rate Li2ZnTi3O8 (LZTO) anode for Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). Here, we reported a synthetic strategy toward in situ growth of homodispersed Li2ZnTi3O8 on ultralight 3D carbon aerogels (CAs) via facile heat treatment and activation process taking LiOH as activating agent. Such an optimized composite obtains a specific surface area of 154.427 m2 g−1 and a high pore volume. The strong interaction between the LZTO and N-doped CAs, a highly electrical conductivity and multidimensional ion transport channels of the hybrid result in a high capacity, outstanding high-rate performance and long cycle life. As a result, the NPC-LZTO electrode (NPC-LZTO-B) delivers a commendable reversible capacity at high-rate (e.g. 162.4 mAh g−1 at 5 A g−1 (22 C) and 127.3 mAh g−1 at 10 A g−1) and durable long-term performance (capacity retention of 73% after 4500 cycles at 5 A g−1). Moreover, the kinetic analysis confirms that the feature of pseudocapacitance boosts lithium-ion storage performance at high rate. Porous carbon aerogels frame constructed LZTO nanocomposite electrodes provide a facile way to design high durable LIBs anode.

ce:section.ce:para: As an indispensable electrochemical energy storage device, Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been extensively used in various fields and greatly facilitate our lives [1–5]. Lithium-ion batteries as power sources for electric vehicles (EVs) and hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) are further optimized at power density and safety to be extremely competitive [6,7]. Therefore, the exploitation of high-performance and high-security electrode materials is an urgent goal. However, commercial graphite anode does not meet the above requirements owing to the security troubles caused by the lithium (Li) dendrite formation and low ionic/electronic diffusion coefficient.

The spinel Li2ZnTi3O8 (LZTO, P4332 space group), a promising anode candidates, featuring satisfied performances and safety is receiving more and more attention [8–10]. In addition, high theoretical capacity (229 mAh g−1), environmental friendliness and low cost make LZTO more suitable for large-scale production [11–13]. Unfortunately, the poor electronic conductivity (10−13 S m−1) and low Li+ diffusion coefficient in bulk LZTO are the major obstacles to obtain an imperfect high rate performance [14]. Therefore, various developed strategies are designed to solve these problems, including morphological engineering [15,16] and coating conductive matrixes [17–28]. Among these methods, designing different structured porous carbon foams/LZTO composites demonstrates different levels of success in the commercial applications [29–35]. Specifically, carbon aerogel, a distinctive discovered material, has significant advantages including hierarchical three-dimensional network, adjusted pore distribution and a high specific surface area [37-39]. Moreover, hierarchical porous structure of carbon aerogel realizing fast mass transfer and acting as a network supporting active material is extremely competitive for high-rate performance LIBs. Studies by Xiang et al. suggested that a nano-Li4Ti5O12/graphene microsphere composite material (called LTO/rGO) can deliver a high reversible capacity of 70.3 mAh g−1 at 200 C [34]. And Yang team in situ synthesized hierarchical mesoporous lithium titanate (LTO)/graphene aerogel hybrids, the unique hybrid structure owns fast ion and electron transmission paths [36]. To further improve Li+ storage performance, heteroatoms (e. g. N, S, P, B) were used to modify carbon matrix for introducing defect structure, more active sites and disordered structure effectively [38–41]. Meng et al. successfully synthesized lithium titanate nanoparticles with N-doped carbon coating (NC/LTO) using chitosan as the carbon and nitrogen sources exhibits a high specific capacity of 194 mAh g−1 at 1 C [41].

In this work, “China rose-like” morphology of 3D carbon foam encapsulating LZTO nanoparticles hybrids (NPC-LZTO) were constructed successfully using carboxymethyl chitosan (CCS) biomass as new carbon precursors. Compared with the usual carbon materials, ultralight 3D carbon aerogels provide fast Li+ transfer channel from electrolyte to electrode surface to improve ion diffusion kinetics. This novel structure benefits to adapt to volume changes in the process of electrochemical reaction and offers a large surface area to increase the accessibility of electrode. In addition, N-doped carbon is formed accompanied by the decomposition of carboxylated chitosan (CCS) at the process of heat treatment, which destroys the electronegativity of the carbon to enhance the electrochemical activity of the electrode and improve the electronic conductivity. As anode materials for LIBs, the NPC-LZTO-B delivers higher discharge capacity, better cycling stability and more excellent high-rate performance than P-LZTO. Therefore, NPC-LZTO-B electrode material with stable performance, simple manufacturing process and low cost provides a broad basis for large scale production.

Briefly, 5 ml of carboxylated chitosan (CCS) (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) aqueous suspension (5 mg ml−1) was added slowly to 50 ml of aqueous suspension of tetrabutyl titanate (TBT, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) (1.2 mg ml−1, 2.4 mg ml−1, 3.6 mg ml−1) under magnetic stirring. After centrifugation and washing with distilled water, the composite of CCS-Ti was obtained. Then, with a molar ratio of Ti:Li:Zn = 3:2.3:1, Lithium hydroxide (LiOH·2H2O, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) (0.276 mg ml−1, 0.552 mg ml−1, 0.828 mg ml−1) and Zinc oxide (ZnO, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) (0.095 mg ml−1, 0.191 mg ml−1, 0.287 mg ml−1) were dissolved in 20 ml of deionized water and stirred continuously until a transparent solution was formed. 0.2 g of CCS-TiO2 were added to the above solution under magnetic stirring to obtain CCS-Ti-Zn-Li.

N-doped porous carbon-LZTO (NPC-LZTO) is prepared by self-assembly between chitosan molecules and CCS-Ti-Zn-Li under acidic conditions, and then combined with freeze-drying and calcination processes. A certain amount of CCS-Ti-Zn-Li powder was dissolved in 1% acetic acid solution at 273 K to form a uniform pregel solution (The pH value in self assembly process is about 4). The pregel solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min to remove air bubbles, and freeze-dried for 48 h. Ultimately, samples were annealed at 800 °C in argon atmosphere for 2 h, washed with deionized water for several times to form 3D porous LZTO/C hybrids, and named as NPC-LZTO-A, NPC-LZTO-B and NPC-LZTO-C. As a comparison, P-LZTO was obtained in the same route without adding CCS and the chitosan carbon aerogel (CA) was prepared in the same route without adding Li, Ti, Zn source.

The size, morphology and microstructure of samples were observed with field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM-7800F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100). Elemental mapping images were collected on a Zeiss supra55 system. The phase structures of samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction measurement (XRD) using the Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer in the region of 2θ = 10–80° at 40 kV/40 mA with Cu K radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The chemical states at the surface of the samples were obtained from X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo VG Scientific, USA) measurement. Raman spectroscopy was collected using Raman spectroscope (HORIBA Scientific Instruments Division). The content of the carbon in the products were determined by the thermogravimetric analyzer (NETZSCH STA 449F3, Germany) under the air atmosphere from 90 °C to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The specific surface areas of samples were measured by adsorption/desorption of N2 (BET, ASAP2460) and the pore size distributions were analyzed by Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model adsorption branch of the isotherms.

Electrochemical evaluation of electrodes was performed with a LIR2032 coin-type battery and the batteries were assembled in a glove box with Ar. At first, the active electrodes were constructed by agitating a mixture of active material (80 wt%), carbon black (10 wt%) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) (10 wt%) in N-methyl-pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent. Then the thick slurry was spread onto a clean copper foil and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for overnight. Finally, the film was tailored into small slice with a diameter of 14 mm and the active mass loading on each electrode was ca.1.2 mg. Polyethylene Celgard 2400 was used as a separator and Li metal was used as the counter and reference electrodes to assemble a coin cell. 1 M LiPF6 was dissolved in DMC/EC/EMC (volume ratio of 1:1:1) as electrolyte. The equipped battery was allowed to stand for 8 h before the measurement. The Land CT2001A tester was used to test the regular electrochemical performance. Constant current charge-discharge and cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were tested at a potential range of 0.02–3 V, and 1 C was equal to a current density of 229 mA g−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopies (EIS) (10−2 Hz–105 Hz) were tested on the CHI660E electrochemical workstation.

The synthesis strategy of NPC-LZTO (NPC-LZTO-A, NPC-LZTO-B, NPC-LZTO-C) includes the process of freeze drying, heat treatment and activation, the schematic diagram is depicted in Fig. 1 . The CCS-Ti-Zn-Li compound ingredients are sufficiently dissolved in 1% glacial acetic acid solution [40]. Then, the formation of hydrogen bonds between the group of the acetic acid molecule and the chitosan molecule contribute to the planar network [39,41,42]. Besides, metal ion particles were dispersed uniformly in the network. During the freeze-drying process, meso/macro-pores are formed [43]. A large number of water molecules serve as macropores and mesoporous templates are limited in the 3D frame and pores are formed when they come out of the aerogel. Then, LZTO nanoparticles are inserted in the carbon skeleton after high-temperature pyrolysis processes. Meantime, LiOH as activating agent promoted the formation of mesopores on carbon nanosheets. In addition, chitosan is rich in NH2 group. The NH2 group decomposes into H and N at high temperature and N stays in the carbon skeleton to form N doped carbon-LZTO composition ultimately [40]. The image of the material preparation process is shown in Fig. S1. The carbon contents of all samples are determined by TG dates. As shown in Fig. S2, the carbon contents of NPC-LZTO-A, NPC-LATO-B and NPC-LZTO-C are 6%, 11% and 19%, respectively.

The morphology of NPC-LZTO-B hybrid is confirmed by Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). Through pyrolysis and activation process at high temperature, LZTO particles in-situ grew on curly nanosheets with uniform distribution (Fig. 2 a and b). As presented in Fig. 2c, LZTO particles are intensively intertwined by the 3D cross-linked carbon aerogel matrix, forming a “China rose-like” composite. The 3D carbon skeleton provides a large specific surface area and fast conductive network. In addition, the metal loading in the carbon matrix can control the morphology of the hybrid. When the metal content further increases, the morphology of NPC-LZTO-A (Fig. S3a–b) collapsed slightly and the surface of the nanosheet were rough and not complete, which will lead to poor long cycle performance. Low-magnification Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) images were further displayed in Fig. 2d-e. It can be noted that NPC-LZTO-B exhibits good dispersibility on nanosheets with the size of 50–100 nm (Fig. 2c). What’s more, the magnified TEM image (Fig. 2e) shows that mesopores hierarchical structure with the size of 10–35 nm appear on the surface of the nanosheets, which mainly relies on the template of ice crystals and activation process of LiOH. This unique mesopores hierarchical structure provides the channels from electrolyte to the electrode surface and favors for electrolyte accessibility. High resolution TEM image (Fig. 2f) indicates the clear lattice fringe assigned to the plan (311) of NPC-LZTO-B. And, the yellow dotted line refers to the amorphous carbon layer dramatically improving the conductivity of LZTO. In addition, the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) element mapping (Fig. 2g) further proves the good dispersion of Ti, Zn, C and N elements on the entire hybrid surface. It is noteworthy that the boundary between carbon matrix and LZTO nanoparticles is distinct.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of all samples are shown in Fig. 3 a. All the main diffraction peaks of NPC-LZTO are well indexed to the cubic spinel structure LZTO (JCPDS card number 86-1512) with space group P4332 [21]. No distinct impurity peaks are found in NPC-LZTO-B, demonstrating that the influence of CCS addition on crystal structure is negligible. As shown in Fig. S4, the confirmed composition of N-CA is amorphous carbon with a graphite structure [46]. An additional peak at 23°, attributed to amorphous carbon, was observed in the XRD diffraction pattern of NPC-LZTO-A, NPC-LZTO-B and NPC-LZTO-C. In addition, the lattice parameters for all samples have been given in Table S1. The lattice parameters for NPC-LZTO-B increased slightly compared with P-LZTO, which is caused by the interstitial doping of carbon atoms and nitrogen atoms [47]. What’ more, the structure and graphitization degree of the carbon hybrids are explored in Fig. 3b. The typical characteristic peaks are located at ∼1350 cm−1 (G band) and ∼1585 cm−1 (D band), corresponding to ordered sp2 carbon stretching vibrations or defects and disordered sp3 bonded [44–46]. As far as we know, the Raman spectra with deconvolutional peaks shows that the strength ratios of D and G (ID/IG) spectra (i.e., the area of D and G spectra) are related to the disorder degree of carbon and the average size of the sp2 structural domain in the composite electrodes [44,45,47]. The area ratios of peak D and peak G are obtained by fitting curves. The ID/IG value of NPC-LZTO-B (2.18) is higher than CA (1.54), revealing that the defects and vacancies of the composite electrode increase, which will provide more active sites for Li+ storage. Obviously, the G and D bands of NPC-LZTO-B are significantly shifted compared with CAs, implying a good electronic interaction between LZTO and CAs [34].

The internal pore size distribution and BET surface area of the material are studied by N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms technology. The BET surface area of NPC-LZTO-B computed by the multi-point Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method is 154.427 m2 g−1 (NPC-LZTO-B), while the BET surface area of P-LZTO is only 1.1149 m2 g−1. A large BET surface area attributed to the porous three-dimensional structure, can increase the electrode/electrolyte reaction interface and further enhance the capacity of NPC-LZTO-B. The adsorption-desorption isotherm of NPC-LZTO-B (Fig. 3c) shows IV-type curve [43]. Meanwhile, the hysteresis loop is close to the H3 type indicating the existence of mesoporous structure. The pores size distribution calculated by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model are shown in Fig. 3d. The pore diameter distribution of NPC-LZTO-B shows that mesopore (nm <50 nm) is dominant, and the macropore (nm >50 nm) takes up a fraction of the volume, which is corresponded to the result of TEM. At the same time, the content of LZTO will directly affect the formation of 3D carbon-coated structure. It is difficult for carbon skeleton to contain a large amount of metal such as NPC-LZTO-A, which has a smaller BET surface area (87.3179 m2 g−1) and the pore volume (0.06056 cm3 g−1). The reasons are that the three-dimensional skeleton structure will be destroyed and collapse and too much metal ion accumulation will further affect the formation of pores in the carbon gel framework. However, with the increase of CCS content in raw materials, the volume of macropore increases obviously of NPC-LZTO-C. It is widely known that a mass of macropores is difficult to form sufficient and adequate ion channels. At the same time, the electrode with an excessive BET surface area of NPC-LZTO-C (207.982 m2 g−1) will produce more side effects and further decrease the tap density. In general, NPA-LZTO-B has the proper pore volume and distribution as well as BET surface area. Such mesoporous hierarchical structure makes for the establishment of crosslinked ion channels and penetration of electrolyte to improve high-rate performance.

The compositional chemical state of the atoms on the surface of NPC-LZTO-B and P-LZTO are further confirmed by XPS and C1s spectrum (284.8 eV) is taken as a reference to check the binding energy of Ti, Zn, O, C, and N in the full spectrum (Fig. 4 a). Fig. 4b exhibits the C1s high-resolution XPS spectrum. The binding energies at 284.7, 285.8, 286.4, and 289.3 eV correspond to CC, CN CO and CCO. The N1s peak can be decomposed into three components (Fig. 4c). The binding energy peaks of 398.1, 399.5 and 402.1 represent the pyridine, pyrrole and graphitization types of N atoms, respectively. The presence of Pyridinic N and Pyrrole N have high electrochemical activity and produce more defects to be expected to increase the capacity of the material [48]. The peaks at binding energies of 400.7 and 407.2 eV are allocated to TiNO and NO3 − radicals and the evolution of NO3 − is beneficial to promote the migration of Li+ [28,49,50]. XPS analysis shows that the N doping level is 1.3% in NPC-LZTO-B. The O 1s spectra of NPC-LZTO-B can be divided into two components at binding energies of TiO (530.5 eV) and CO (532.8 eV), respectively (Fig. 4d). The binding energies of TiO for P-LZTO (530.8 eV) and NPC-LZTO-B (530.5 eV) is deviated deriving from the reassignment of O charge. The high-resolution spectrum of Ti 2p (Fig. 4e) shows two peaks near 465.1 eV and 459.3 eV, corresponding to the characteristic bands Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p2/3, respectively, and expected TiNO peak is 459.1 eV, which improves intrinsic conductivity of LZTO. The peaks of Zn 2p with binding energies of 1021.5 and 1044.8 are related to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p2/3 (Fig. 4f). Significantly, carbonation and activation processes will bring about the modification of chemical environment for Ti4+ and Zn2+. And for this reason, Ti 2p and Zn 2p peaks for NPC-LZTO-B shift to low binding energies reference to those of P-LZTO.

For the sake of investigating the reversibility and kinetic characteristics of NPC-LZTO-B and P-LZTO, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were conducted at a scan rate of 0.2 mV s−1(Fig. 5 a). The oxidation peak and the reduction peak are located at 1.6 V and 1.3 V, which represents the oxidation-reduction reaction of Ti4+/Ti3+ [28]. And another reduction peak can be observed around 0.5 V, mainly due to multiple reduction reactions of Ti4+. Compared with the P-LZTO, NPC-LZTO-B manifests the sharper redox peaks and the smaller potential gap between reduction and oxidation peaks (0.362 V < 0.428 V), which originates from smaller polarization behavior and best dynamic characteristics. The improvement may be ascribed to mesoporous hierarchical carbon aerogel, which enhances electrical conductivity. The rate performance and charge/discharge profiles of as-prepared materials are shown in Fig. 5b, c and S5. All electrodes were measured at increasing current densities from 0.5 to 5 A g−1 with potential window of 0.02–3 V. The NPC-LZTO-B electrode displays excellent rate performance and a high capacity of 246, 212.2, 197.4, 191.3, 180.2 and 163.7 mAh g−1 at 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 A g−1, respectively. In addition, when the current density returns to 0.5 A g−1, the capacity is almost back to the original value, which exhibits superior dynamic characteristic. As a comparison, owing to poor electron and ionic conductivity, the capacity of P-LZTO damping rapidly as the current density increasing and owns only discharge capacity of 38 mAh g−1 at 5 A g−1. As shown in Fig. S6, N-CA displays a low discharge capacity of 59.1 mAh g−1 at the current density of 2 A g−1 with potential window of 0.02–3 V. Thus, the contribution of capacity does not belong to the N-CA. For the NPC-LZTO-B composite, the unique structure of 3D carbon foam supplying interconnected conductive network to encapsulate LZTO nanoparticles, realizes fast and multidimensional migration paths for ion and electron. Therefore, compared with LZTO, NPC-LZTO-B shows better stability and higher specific capacity at high rates. To further highlight the excellent superhigh rate performance, the NPC-LZTO-B electrode was continue tested at higher density from 6 (26.4 C) to 10 A g −1 (44 C). It can be noted that NPC-LZTO-B (Fig. S8) obtains a high specific capacity and excellent stability. Even at the current density of 10 A g−1, a high specific capacity of 127.3 mAh g−1 can also be achieved. In addition, it is noteworthily that the capacity retention rate of NPC-LZTO-B is 100% when the current density returns to 6 A g−1. The function between reversible capacity and current density of the latest works are shown in Fig. 5d. The electrode materials prepared by these teams basically have nanostructure or choose conductive materials as support to improve electronic conductivity, but the high-rate performance is not satisfied. Compared with these works, the NPC-LZTO-B electrode material with complementary characteristics has 3D conductive carbon skeleton and porous structure, which are useful in constructing ion channels and increasing electrical conductivity. Hence, NPC-LZTO-B electrode exhibits high specific capacities and excellent stability at the current density from 0.5 to 10 A g−1, while high-rate capacity (current density >5 A g−1) of other related work are not reported. Furthermore, as demonstrated in Fig. 5e, the long cycling performance of NPC-LZTO-B and P-LZTO were further explored at 5 A g−1. After activation at a small current density of 50 mA g−1 for the initial cycle, the NPC-LZTO-B exhibits outstanding cyclic stability with a high specific capacity (163.5 mAh g−1) at the second cycle and the capacity retention of 75.9% after 3000 cycles. Further, NPC-LZTO-B retains a perfect specific capacity of 120.3 mAh g−1 (retention rate of 73%) after 4500 cycles, which is equivalent to a capacity attenuation of 0.006% per cycle. On the contrary, P-LZTO shows a low discharge capacity (133.9 mAh g−1) at the second cycle and loses almost all capacity after 3000 cycles. The reason for the stable cycling performance of NPC-LZTO-B is the tight connection between CAs foam and LZTO particles. In this intimate framework, the “China rose-like” morphology of 3D foam encapsulating LZTO buffers volume changes and keeps structure integrity. The porous carbon foam provides ion transport in the cycling process, leading to the high-rate cycling performance of NPC-LZTO-B electrode. The low temperature performance is a vital factor for commercial application of LIBs. The result of NPC-LZTO-B at low temperature of 0 °C, −10 °C and −20 °C is shown in Fig. S9. Firstly, the button battery was tested for 10 cycles at a current density of 0.1 A g−1 at a room temperature of 25 °C for activation, and then cycled for 60 cycles at 0 °C, −10 °C and −20 °C. The discharge capacity of NPC-LZTO-B are 315.7, 237.3, 185.3, and 175.9 mAh g−1 at 26 °C, 0 °C, −10 °C and −20 °C, respectively. In conclusion, the excellent low temperature performance of NPC-LZTO-B electrode owns to good structural stability.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy tests were conducted for as-prepared electrodes in the frequency range of 10−2 Hz–105 Hz (Fig. 6 a) to investigate the effect of carbon materials on the diffusion kinetics behavior of LZTO. The point at the intersection of Quist diagram and Z′ axis is the internal resistance of electrolyte in LIBs, namely the ohmic resistance (Rs). The semicircle in the high frequency region indicates the charge transfer resistance (Rct) related to the SEI film and a smaller value of Rct means a faster charge transfer process. The line in the low frequency region associates with the diffusion of Li+ in the system. The equivalent circuit diagram (Fig. 6a illustration) matches the EIS spectrum very well, and date in scatter plot are fitted by ZSimWin software. Next, the diffusion coefficient of Li+ is calculated by formulas (1) and (2).
(1)
D
L
i +
=
( R T )
2
2
( A n
F 2
C
L
i
+ σ
)
2
(2)
Z ′
=
R 1
+
R 2
+ σ
ω
− 0.5
Where R is the gas constant, T is the absolute temperature, F is the Faraday constant, and the number of electron transfer in the Li+ intercalation-deintercalation process is 1. In this work, Li+ molality is CLi + and electrode surface area is A. There is a linear relationship between ω and Z′, and Warburg factor (σ) can be obtained from the slope of the line Z′-ω−1/2 (Fig. 6b). The values of R Ω , Rct, DLi + are shown in Table S3. The result shows that NPC-LZTO-B electrolyte displays minimum R Ω (7.05 Ω) and Rct (71.2 Ω). In addition, NPC-LZTO-B electrolyte exhibits maximum DLi +(2.029 × 10−10 cm2 s−1), which is higher than that of the P-LZTO. This result is well agreed with the excellent high-rate performance of the materials. Good kinetic performance can be explained by the fact that the N-doped porous composite electrode has better electrolyte permeability and enhanced conductivity.

The robust structure of NPC-LZTO-B electrode after 1000 cycles at 5 A g−1 is further analyzed through SEM and XRD tests. The SEM images of NPC-LZTO-B are demonstrated before cycling (Fig. 7 a) and after 1000 cycles (Fig. 7b). Li2ZnTi3O8 nanoparticles are tightly wrapped by the carbon skeleton and “China rose-like” morphology is well retained after long cycles. Moreover, the result of XRD tests (Fig. 7c-d) display that the crystal structure of NPC-LZTO-B is not changed, which reveals the amazing Li+ diffusion reaction kinetics and good electrode reversibility. Base on the discussion above, the schematic illustration of the relationship between excellent Li+ storage performance and turdy construction is displayed in.

Fig. 7e. For raw LZTO nanoparticles, exposed surface will react with the electrolyte to form a thick SEI film during lithiation/delithiation cycling, which leads to increased polarization and damage electrical conductivity of external circuits. While, for NPC-LZTO-B, the uniform surface carbon coating, protects LZTO against direct contact with electrolyte to avoid side reactions. In addition, the unique 3D porous structure of NPC-LZTO-B can enhance the interaction between the LZTO and CAs foam and provide extra space to accommodate changes of volume. The above excellent characteristics ensure the electrochemical stability and high-rate performance of NPC-LZTO-B.

CV dynamics analysis was carried out to explore the reasons of the excellent lithium storage performance of NCP-LZTO-B during the rapid charge-discharge period. The typical CV curves of NCP-LZTO-B and P-LZTO with different scan speeds from 0.2 to 1.2 mV s−1 were shown in Fig. 8 a and Fig. 8b. Significant broad peaks were observed at the cathode peak and anode peak, and with the increase of the rate, the redox peak shows a smaller voltage offset, indicating that the NPC-LZTO-B electrode material has fast kinetics. The relationship between peak current (ip) and the square root of the scan rate (ν1/2) are shown in Fig. 8c. According to the Randles−Sevcik equation (Text S2, Supporting Information), the Li+ diffusion coefficients (D0) of NPC-LZTO-B and P-LZTO are calculated and the results are shown in Table S5. NPC-LZTO-B with higher Li+ diffusion coefficients exhibits excellent electrochemical performance. In the redox reaction, the charge transfer behavior of the surface atoms and reversible charge storage (pseudocapacitance) of the double layer are mainly included in the cell. To determine the ion storage type of NCP-LZTO-B, the response between current density (I) and scanning rate (V) was expressed by the typical equation I = a V b. The value of B is determined by the change curve between log (I) and log (V), and the charge storage type is distinguished by the value of B: the charge is controlled by diffusion (b = 0.5) and total charge comes from the capacitive process (b = 1). The values of B shown in Fig. 8d are 0.63 and 0.87, respectively, which indicates that the NCP-LZTO-B electrode has two properties: capacitor and battery. According to formula (3) and (4), the contribution rate of pseudocapacitance to total charge storage can be calculated.
(3)
i =
k 1
v +
k 2
v 0.5
(4)
i
( V )
/
v 0.5
=
k 1
v 0.5
+
k 2

Where, V represents specific scanning rate, K1 and K2 are adjustable parameters that can be obtained by fitting, and K1 V −1/2 represents the contribution of surface capacitance. Fig. 8e shows the capacity contribution of NCP-LZTO-B under the rate of 1.2 mV s−1, where the charge coming from capacitance contribution accounts for 53.69%. In addition, the contribution rate of pseudo-capacitance increases with the increase of scanning rate (Fig. 8f). Under the rapid charge and discharge phase, the diffusion dynamics of Li+ is limited, while the interface storage dynamics is still praiseworthy. Therefore, according to the dynamic analysis, the dynamic behavior of NCP-LZTO-B is controlled by NCP-LZTO-B-electrolyte and NCP-LZTO-B-carbon materials interface, which also explains the excellent high-rate ability of NCP-LZTO-B.

In summary, chitosan was successfully applied to prepare N self-doped carbon aerogel-LZTO anode via facile heat treatment and activation process taking LiOH as Li source and activating agent. The Li2ZnTi3O8 nanoparticles integrated on mesoporous hierarchical carbon foam construct continuous and multidimensional channels to provide extra space to accommodate changes of volume and fast ion/electron transport. Owing to the unique architecture, NPC-LZTO-B demonstrates a high capacity of 127.3 mAh g−1 at 10 A g−1 (44 C). For the first time, the ultra-long cycle performance of 4500 cycles at a current density of 5 A g−1 for NPC-LZTO-B is reported. CV dynamics analysis shows that behaviors of diffusion and pseudocapacitance in the battery take effect simultaneously for Li+ storage. Presumably, the construction strategy of this study can be extended to the preparation of other energy storage functional materials for high performance LIBs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (19DZ2271100).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jingjing Wang is a graduatestudent at Shanghai University of Electric Power. She focuses her researches on advanced materials for energy conversion and storage.
Wenhan Zhang is a graduatestudent at Shanghai University of Electric Power. His current research focuses on nanomaterials for rechargeable batteries.
Jiawei Li is a graduatestudent at Shanghai University of Electric Power. His research direction is lithium ion battery cathode material.
Bin Wang is a graduatestudent at Shanghai University of Electric Power. His research focuses on cathode materials for advanced lithium-ion batteries.
Chao Xu is a graduatestudent at Shanghai University of Electric Power. His is interested in designing high performance materials used in batteries.

Chunyan La is a professor at Shanghai University of Electric Power, China. She received her Ph.D. degree in Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology at 2006. Her major research mainly focused on the synthesis of functional materials, and their applications in Lithium ion batteries.
